courses.semester: Second Half of Year (H - begins week 7)
courses.prerequisites: Permission of the Dean or Head of Department required prior to registration. Intended for students advised to transfer from CSC1015F after week 5 assessment. Equivalent to CSC1015F in level, credit value and as prerequisite for certain other courses. Note: Credit will not be given for CSC1015F/S and CSC1016S together with CSC1010H and CSC1011H.
courses.course_outline: This course is an introduction to problem solving, algorithm development and programming in the Python language. It includes fundamental programming constructs and abstractions, sorting and searching techniques, and machine representations of data. The practical component covers input/output, conditionals, loops, strings, functions, arrays, lists, dictionaries, recursion, text files and exceptions in Python. Students are taught testing and debugging, as well as sorting and searching algorithms, algorithm complexity and equivalence classes. Number systems, binary arithmetic, boolean algebra and logic gates are also introduced. The course places an emphasis on the strengthening of foundational concepts and skills, the carefully-paced introduction of new material, and the development of sound approaches to effective learning.
courses.lecture_times: Monday - Friday, 5th period. Tutorials: One per week, replacing one lecture. Practicals: One per week, Thursday, 14h00 - 17h30.
courses.dp_requirements: Minimum of 45% aggregate in practical work.
courses.assessment: Theory tests count 15%; practical tests and practical assignments count 25%; one 2-hour examination written in November counts 60%. Subminima: 45% for practicals, 45% on weighted average of theory tests and examination.

courses.semester: Full Year (H - follows CSC1010H)
courses.prerequisites: CSC1010H. Equivalent to CSC1016S in level, credit value and as prerequisite for certain other courses.
courses.course_outline: The first half of the course aims to further develop problem solving and programming in Python. The second half focuses on object-oriented design and programming in Java, covering object-oriented design techniques and UML diagrams, as well as elementary data structures such as lists, stacks and queues, as well as introducing important considerations relating to Human Computer Interaction and interface design. The practical component includes use of inheritance, polymorphism, interfaces, generics and GUI programming in Java.
courses.lecture_times: Monday - Thursday, 4th period. Tutorials: One per week, replacing one lecture. Practicals: One per week, Monday, 14h00 - 16h00.
courses.dp_requirements: Minimum of 45% aggregate in practical work.
courses.assessment: Theory tests count 25%; practical tests and practical assignments count 25%; one 2-hour examination written in November counts 50%. Subminima: 45% for practicals, 45% on weighted average of theory tests and examination.

courses.semester: First or Second Semester (F/S)
courses.prerequisites: At least 70% for NSC Mathematics. Students assessed in week 5; if not coping may be required to transfer to CSC1010H from week 7. Note: Credit will not be given for CSC1015F/S and CSC1016S together with CSC1010H and CSC1011H.
courses.course_outline: This course is an introduction to problem solving, algorithm development and programming in the Python language. It includes fundamental programming constructs and abstractions, sorting and searching techniques, and machine representations of data. The practical component covers input/output, conditionals, loops, strings, functions, arrays, lists, dictionaries, recursion, text files and exceptions in Python. Students are taught testing and debugging, as well as sorting and searching algorithms, algorithm complexity and equivalence classes. Number systems, binary arithmetic, Boolean algebra and logic gates are also introduced. The course is offered in a blended-learning format: students are provided with a set of online video lectures and contact time is in a tutorial/practical format aimed at reinforcing the principles introduced in the online lectures and giving students time to do exercises under the supervision of tutors.
courses.lecture_times: 4th or 5th period once per week. Tutorials: One per week, replacing one lecture. Practicals: One per week, Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday or Thursday, 14h00 - 16h00 or 16h00 - 18h00.
courses.dp_requirements: Minimum of 45% aggregate in practical work.
courses.assessment: Theory tests 15%; practical tests and practical assignments 25%; June examination 2 hours 60%. Subminima: 45% for practicals, 45% on weighted average of theory tests and examination.

courses.semester: Second Semester (S)
courses.prerequisites: CSC1015F (or at least 60% for CSC1017S).
courses.course_outline: This course builds on the foundation of CSC1015F/CSC1010H, with a focus on object-oriented design and programming in Java, as well as introducing important considerations relating to Human Computer Interaction and interface design. The Java component of the course covers object-oriented design techniques and UML diagrams, as well as elementary data structures such as lists, stacks and queues. The practical component includes use of inheritance, polymorphism, interfaces, generics and GUI programming in Java.
courses.lecture_times: 4th or 5th period daily. Tutorials: One per week, replacing one lecture. Practicals: One per week, Monday, Tuesday or Wednesday, 14h00 - 16h00 or 16h00 - 18h00.
courses.dp_requirements: Minimum of 45% aggregate in practical work.
courses.assessment: Theory tests count 15%; practical tests and practical assignments count 25%; one 2-hour exam written in November counts 60%. Subminima: 45% for practicals and 45% on weighted average of theory tests and examination.

courses.semester: First Semester (F)
courses.prerequisites: (CSC1015F/S and CSC1016S) or (CSC1010H and CSC1011H). Note: A laptop is required for use during class sessions and after hours; minimum specifications available at https://internal.cs.uct.ac.za/teaching/laptop_requirement.html.
courses.course_outline: This course builds on the first year Computer Science foundation with an emphasis on data storage and manipulation. The course covers abstract data types and assertions, recursive algorithms, tree structures such as AVL and B-trees, graph traversals, minimum spanning trees, sets, hashing and priority queues. An introduction to conceptual modelling, database design and relational database manipulation is included. Practical programming in Java in a Unix environment is an important part of the course.
courses.lecture_times: Monday - Friday, 2nd period. Four or five lectures per week. Practicals: One 4-hour practical per week, Monday - Friday, 14h00 - 18h00.
courses.dp_requirements: Minimum of 45% aggregate in practical work.
courses.assessment: Tests 20%; practicals and practical test 30%; exam 50%. Subminima: 45% on weighted average of theory tests and examination.

courses.semester: Second Semester (S)
courses.prerequisites: CSC2001F (at least 45% for CSC2001F). Note: A laptop is required for use during class sessions and after hours.
courses.course_outline: The aim of this course is to build on the foundational concepts covered in CSC2001F, with further necessary core topics of an undergraduate Computer Science curriculum. These topics comprise: concurrent and parallel computing (including practical work in Java); computer architecture; an introduction to Society, Ethics, and the Profession (covering ethical issues such as property rights, freedom of expression and privacy, free and open source software); and Theory of Computing (including Turing Machines and the limits of computation).
courses.lecture_times: Monday - Friday, 2nd period. Four lectures per week. Practicals: One 4-hour practical per week, Monday - Friday, 14h00 - 18h00.
courses.dp_requirements: Minimum of 45% aggregate in practical work.
courses.assessment: Tests 20%; practicals and practical test 30%; exam 50%. Subminima: 45% on weighted average of theory tests and examination.

courses.semester: Not specified (Z)
courses.prerequisites: (CSC1015F/S and CSC1016S) or (CSC1010H and CSC1011H). Note: Required for all students majoring in Computer Science and/or wishing to continue to any third-year CS courses. Compulsory course in the Computer Science major CSC05.
courses.course_outline: All students who take advanced courses in Computer Science need to build on a foundation of strong programming skills. The aim of this course is to assess and confirm mastery in fundamental programming skills before students can proceed to advanced courses.
courses.assessment: Practical programming examination counts for 100%.

courses.semester: First or Second Semester (F/S)
courses.prerequisites: Academically strong students may apply. Selection based on final results in CSC1015F, CSC1016S and CSC2001F. Places limited depending on availability of supervisors; final decision at discretion of Head of Department.
courses.course_outline: This course allows students to pursue a course of independent research in one of the areas of specialisation of the department under the direct supervision of one of the staff members. Students will learn research methods in Computer Science and apply these in a suitable project. They will also learn about research writing (proposal and report). Students will complete a research project and document this in a research report (mini-dissertation). An intermediate deliverable will be a project proposal and presentation to staff.
courses.lecture_times: Regular meetings with supervisor, by arrangement.
courses.assessment: Proposal 20%, Final research report 80%.

courses.semester: First Semester (F)
courses.prerequisites: CSC1015F/S, CSC1016S, MAM1031F (or equivalent), MAM1032S (or equivalent), (MAM1019H or STA1000X or MAM1008S). Note: A laptop is required.
courses.course_outline: The course introduces the field of Artificial Intelligence and then covers the fundamentals of knowledge representation and reasoning as a paradigm for Artificial Intelligence. It includes relevant topics such as logical agents and the creation of symbolic and logical representations from problem statements. Inference in propositional and first-order logic are used to build upon the fundamentals of computational logic and automated reasoning. An overview of core algorithms for knowledge representation (e.g., tableau, case-based, constraints, SAT solving) is given. Approaches to handling uncertainty in knowledge representation and reasoning are introduced through random variables, probabilistic inference, and the framework of utility functions for decision making.
courses.lecture_times: Monday - Friday, 3rd period. Four lectures per week. Practicals: One 4-hour practical per week, Monday - Friday, 14h00 - 18h00.
courses.dp_requirements: Minimum of 45% aggregate in practical work.
courses.assessment: Tests 25%; practicals and practical test 25%; examination 50%. Subminima: 45% on weighted average of theory tests and examination.

courses.semester: Second Semester (S)
courses.prerequisites: CSC1015F/S, CSC1016S, MAM1031F or MAM1004F or equivalent. Note: A laptop is required.
courses.course_outline: Machine learning is introduced as a paradigm for Artificial Intelligence. Fundamental machine learning algorithms are introduced, and students implement a subset of those using the Python programming language and modern machine learning frameworks in Python. There is a focus on problem formulation, feature representations and evaluation.
courses.lecture_times: Monday - Friday, 3rd period. Four lectures per week. Practicals: One 4-hour practical per week, Monday - Friday, 14h00 - 18h00.
courses.dp_requirements: Minimum of 45% aggregate in practical work.
courses.assessment: Tests 25%; practicals and practical test 25%; examination 50%. Subminima: 45% on weighted average of theory tests and examination.

courses.semester: First Semester (F)
courses.prerequisites: CSC2001F, CSC2002S, CSC2004Z and ((MAM1004F+MAM1008S) or (MAM1000W) or (MAM1031F or equivalent)). Note: A laptop is required.
courses.course_outline: The course provides an introduction to both computer networks, including various logical layers of the ISO OSI layers and network security, and the structure and function of modern operating systems.
courses.lecture_times: Monday - Friday, 2nd period. Practicals: Two 4-hour practicals per week, Monday - Friday, 14h00 - 18h00.
courses.dp_requirements: Minimum of 45% aggregate in practical work.
courses.assessment: Tests 20%; 15% for practicals, 15% for practical tests; exams 50%. Subminima: 45% for practicals; 45% on weighted average of theory tests and examinations.

courses.semester: Second Semester (S)
courses.prerequisites: CSC2001F, CSC2002S, CSC2004Z and ((MAM1004F+MAM1008S) or (MAM1000W) or (MAM1031F or equivalent)). Note: A laptop is required.
courses.course_outline: This course covers three topics in advanced programming: software development, functional programming and the theory of algorithms (how algorithms are categorised, interesting algorithms in each category and how to analyse their complexity).
courses.lecture_times: Monday - Friday, 2nd period. Practicals: Two 4-hour practicals per week, Monday - Friday, 14h00 - 18h00.
courses.dp_requirements: Minimum of 45% aggregate in practical work.
courses.assessment: Tests 20%; practical work and practical tests 30%; exams 50%. Subminima: 45% for practicals; 45% on weighted average of theory tests and examination; 35% for the advanced computing module (comprising Theory of Algorithms and Functional Programming) in the final examination.

courses.semester: Second Semester (S)
courses.prerequisites: CSC2001F, CSC2002S, CSC2004Z and ((MAM1004F+MAM1008S) or (MAM1000W) or (MAM1031F or equivalent)).
courses.course_outline: This course introduces the C++ programming language. Students are exposed to different aspects of modern C++, including pointers, stream operations, RAII, operator overloading, templates and effective use of the standard template library (STL). The course also examines the C++ memory model and shows how this works with move semantics to produce efficient code.
courses.lecture_times: Monday - Friday, 3rd period. Practicals: Two 4-hour practicals per week, by arrangement.
courses.dp_requirements: Minimum of 45% aggregate in practical work.
courses.assessment: 20% tests; 30% practicals and practical tests; 50% final examination. Subminima: 45% practical work; 45% on weighted average of tests and final examination.

courses.semester: First Semester (F)
courses.prerequisites: (CSC2041F or CSC2042S), CSC2001F.
courses.course_outline: This course provides an introduction to problem solving by searching: uninformed search, heuristic search, and adversarial search for games are covered. Introduction to reinforcement learning: Markov Decision Processes for sequential decision making, passive and active reinforcement learning, policy search and data-driven control techniques. The course also covers applications in game playing, planning in interactive environments, and robot navigation and control.
courses.lecture_times: Monday - Friday, 3rd period. Practicals: One 4-hour practical per week, by arrangement.
courses.dp_requirements: Minimum of 45% aggregate in practical work.
courses.assessment: Tests 25%; practical work 25%; examinations 50%. Subminima: 45% for practicals, 45% weighted average of theory tests and examinations.

courses.semester: First Semester (F)
courses.prerequisites: CSC2001F, CSC2042S.
courses.course_outline: The course builds on fundamental concepts in machine learning to introduce modern practical deep neural networks. Different neural network architectures are covered, including deep feedforward networks, convolutional and recurrent neural networks, and Transformers. There is a focus on the design and implementation of scalable deep learning systems. A selection of applications from Natural Language Processing and Computer Vision are included. Finally, ethics for machine learning is introduced through a number of case studies, covering issues such as dataset and algorithmic bias.
courses.lecture_times: Monday - Friday, 3rd period. Practicals: One 4-hour practical per week, by arrangement.
courses.dp_requirements: Minimum of 45% aggregate in practical work.
courses.assessment: Tests 25%; practical work 25%; examinations 50%. Subminima: 45% for practicals, 45% weighted average of theory tests and examinations.

courses.semester: Second Semester (S)
courses.prerequisites: CSC2041F, CSC2042S, CSC2001F, CSC3042F.
courses.course_outline: This course covers a selection of advanced topics in reasoning in Artificial Intelligence such as: reasoning through language with Large Language Models; visual reasoning with diffusion models; probabilistic reasoning with Bayesian networks; logic programming; and expert systems.
courses.lecture_times: Monday - Friday, 3rd period. Practicals: One 4-hour practical per week, by arrangement.
courses.dp_requirements: Minimum of 45% aggregate in practical work.
courses.assessment: Tests 25%; practical work 25%; examinations 50%. Subminima: 45% for practicals, 45% weighted average of theory tests and examinations.

courses.semester: Second Semester (S)
courses.prerequisites: CSC2001F, CSC2041F, CSC2042S, (CSC3041F or CSC3042F).
courses.course_outline: This course provides an overview of agent architectures: cognitive architectures; software agents such as collaborative and information-gathering agents; as well as human-agent interaction and societal impacts of AI systems. Students will apply these concepts through a practical AI system development capstone project.
courses.lecture_times: Monday - Friday, 3rd period. Practicals: One 4-hour practical per week, by arrangement.
courses.dp_requirements: Minimum of 45% aggregate in practical work.
courses.assessment: Tests 25%; practical work 25%; examinations 50%. Subminima: 45% for practicals, 45% weighted average of theory tests and examinations.

courses.semester: Full Year (W)
courses.prerequisites: BSc degree with a major in Computer Science from UCT. An overall pass for the coursework component of this degree.
courses.course_outline: This course is the compulsory research project component of a Computer Science Honours degree. The research project comprises a large project run over the course of the year under academic supervision, with a final mini-dissertation and other project deliverables.
courses.assessment: Not specified in handbook; forms part of the overall Honours degree assessment.

courses.course_title: Selected Honours Module in Computer Science
courses.semester: Not specified (Z). Note: This course will not be offered every year.
courses.prerequisites: Permission from the course convener.
courses.course_outline: This course introduces advanced and cutting edge topics in Computer Science as they emerge with new areas of investigation or practice.
courses.assessment: Exam: 50% and Coursework: 50%.

courses.course_title: Advanced Topics in Computer Science Honours 2
courses.semester: Not specified (Z). Note: This course will not be offered every year.
courses.prerequisites: Permission from the course convener.
courses.course_outline: The course aims to introduce students to advanced and cutting edge topics in Computer Science as they emerge as new areas of investigation or practice, and expose students to new research specialisations in the department. Students will obtain an advanced theoretical understanding of the topic and the ability to apply practically skills learnt related to this specific topic.
courses.assessment: Exam: 50% and Coursework: 50%.

courses.semester: Not specified (Z). Note: This course will not be offered every year.
courses.prerequisites: Admission to BSc Hons specialising in Computer Science, or permission of the course convener.
courses.course_outline: Visualisation is the graphical representation of data with the goal of improving comprehension, communication, hypothesis generation and decision making. This course aims to teach the principles of effective visualisation of large, multidimensional data sets. We cover the field of visual thinking, outlining current understanding of human perception and demonstrating how we can use this knowledge to create more effective data visualisations.
courses.assessment: Students will be assessed with assignments (50%) and an exam (50%). A sub-minimum of 40% will be required for each of the assignment and exam components of the course.

courses.semester: Not specified (Z)
courses.prerequisites: Admission to BSc Hons specialising in Computer Science.
courses.course_outline: This course introduces students to knowledge essential for computer professionals and researchers. The course develops communication and writing skills and introduces basic research methodology. The first module focuses on Professional Communications in general, including written and visual communication. A second component teaches entrepreneurship as New Venture Planning: introducing students to the ideas, theories and concepts associated with entrepreneurial ventures, with a focus on the elements needed to develop a viable business plan. A third module teaches scientific writing and research methods for statistical analysis and evaluation of data.
courses.assessment: Practical aspects evaluated through: a series of 4 NVP assignments (10%/15%/35%/40%), culminating in a business plan; a literature review for the project (35%) and the project proposal (40%). NVP and Research Methods are equally weighted.

courses.semester: Not specified (Z)
courses.prerequisites: Admission to BSc Hons specialising in Computer Science.
courses.course_outline: This course will expose students to the alternative functional programming paradigm, its theoretical underpinnings in the lambda calculus and its practical implementation in specific languages. Students' theoretical understanding of computability will be expanded from the introduction in the undergraduate theory of algorithms module. Students will be introduced to the notion of 'functions as rules'. Topics include side effect free programming and its benefits; first-class functions and higher-order functions; partial application and defining higher-order operations on aggregates (map, reduce/fold, and filter); lazy evaluation and monads.
courses.assessment: Final examination: 50%; Practical assignments: 50%.

courses.semester: Not specified (Z)
courses.prerequisites: Admission to BSc Hons specialising in Computer Science or Information Technology.
courses.course_outline: This course will introduce students to the inner mechanics of a modern programming language compiler or interpreter. Students will appreciate why programming languages are designed in particular ways and they will learn how to develop compilers and compiler-related tools. Course content includes: language classes, formal grammars, recursive descent parsing, tokenisers, parsing, and abstract syntax trees.
courses.assessment: Final examination: 60%; Practical assignments: 40%.

courses.semester: Not specified (Z). Note: This course will not be offered every year.
courses.prerequisites: Admission to BSc Hons specialising in Computer Science or Information Technology.
courses.course_outline: This course will introduce students to the inner mechanics of a modern programming language compiler or interpreter. Students will appreciate why programming languages are designed in particular ways and they will learn how to develop compilers and compiler-related tools. Course content includes: semantic analysis, activation records, intermediate code, optimisations, basic block analysis, instruction selection, liveness analysis and register allocation.
courses.assessment: Final examination: 60%; Practical assignments: 40%.

courses.semester: Not specified (Z). Note: This course will NOT be offered in 2026.
courses.prerequisites: Admission to BSc Hons specialising in Computer Science or Information Technology. A basic understanding of databases, similar to the CSC2001F database material, is expected.
courses.course_outline: This course will enable students to understand the challenges of designing and implementing database applications at very large scale. Topics covered include NoSQL, Hadoop, HBase, HIVE, YARN and Apache Spark, as well as an introduction to data mining techniques and tools. The course concludes with a series of short presentations on new developments in database technology such as spatial, temporal, mobile, multimedia, text and social network data management.
courses.assessment: Final examination: 60%; Practical assignments: 40%.

courses.semester: Not specified (Z). Note: This course will not be offered every year.
courses.prerequisites: Admission to BSc Hons specialising in Computer Science or Information Technology.
courses.course_outline: This course will introduce students to basic concepts and practice around user-centred design of digital systems. Topics include the design cycle, sketching and storyboarding, task analysis, contextual inquiry, conceptual models, usability inspection, human information processing, experience design, and qualitative and quantitative study design and evaluation. Guest speakers from industry and research may be invited.
courses.assessment: Participation: 5%; Individual Practical Assessments: 15%; Group Project Assessments: 30%; Final Exam: 50%.

courses.semester: Not specified (Z). Note: This course will not be offered every year.
courses.prerequisites: Admission to BSc Hons specialising in Computer Science or Information Technology.
courses.course_outline: This course will expose students to foundational concepts and computational techniques in modern Artificial Intelligence and their theoretical underpinnings in logic, search, optimisation and mathematical statistics. Students will also learn how to select and implement these techniques to solve various real world problems. Core topics include: problem solving, knowledge representation and reasoning, machine learning and dealing with uncertainty, with selected topics from: planning, agents and natural language processing.
courses.assessment: Final examination: 60%; Practical assignments: 40%.

courses.semester: Not specified (Z). Note: This course will not be offered every year.
courses.prerequisites: Admission to BSc Hons specialising in Computer Science or Information Technology. Computer Networking at third year level.
courses.course_outline: The objective of this course is to introduce cryptographic techniques and protocols for secure exchange of information on networks and internetworks, and to examine the deployment of these in emerging technologies. Topics include: risk issues (ISO27000; PoPI act); security services; conventional encryption (classical encryption techniques, DES/AES, key distribution, key generation); public-key encryption (RSA algorithm, key management, certification hierarchies); authentication and digital signatures; authentication and key exchange (Kerberos, Diffie-Hellman); electronic messaging security (S-MIME/PGP/WhatsApp); HTTP security (S-HTTP, SSL, capabilities); secure electronic commerce (SET); web application security (OWASP); web-services security (WS-Security, SAML); cloud computing security; critical infrastructure security; Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) and next generation Security Operation Centres.
courses.assessment: Final examination: 60%; Practical assignments: 40%.

courses.semester: Not specified (Z). Note: This course will not be offered every year.
courses.prerequisites: Admission to BSc Hons specialising in Computer Science or Information Technology. Computer Graphics at third year level.
courses.course_outline: This course will introduce students to the techniques and technologies used in designing and programming computer games and related applications. It introduces high-level game programming concepts and practical game construction. Course content include: terminology, methods and tools for computer game development; fundamental algorithms for 2D game development; design and development of simple 3D and networked games; uncertainty and constantly changing gaming environments; and techniques for multiuser and distributed games. Students collaborate with designers and artists to produce a full game.
courses.assessment: Final examination: 60%; Practical assignments: 40%.

courses.semester: Not specified (Z). Note: This course will NOT be offered in 2026.
courses.prerequisites: Admission to BSc Hons specialising in Computer Science or Information Technology.
courses.course_outline: High performance computing is high-speed computing — the use of parallel processing to run application programs efficiently, reliably and fast. Topics covered include: an introduction to and motivation for HPC; HPC architectures with a focus on current supercomputer design; programming interfaces for parallel computing (OpenMP and MPI); parallel libraries; parallel algorithms; benchmarking, profiling and debugging; and case studies of papers from the annual SuperComputing conference.
courses.assessment: Final examination: 50%; Coursework: 50%. Subminima of 45% for both exam and the assignments.

courses.semester: Not specified (Z). Note: This course will not be offered every year.
courses.prerequisites: Admission to BSc Hons specialising in Computer Science or Information Technology.
courses.course_outline: This course will expose students to the foundational theory of 3D computer graphics and provide a short introduction to OpenGL and GLSL shader programming. Theory will encompass the formal description of 3D models and how these can be lit and rendered to produce a desired representation of a 3D scene. Topics include: the rendering pipeline and rasterization; 3D mesh model representation; homogeneous coordinates; modelling and viewing transformations; shading, lighting and texturing; GLSL shader programming; OpenGL/WebGL programming; advanced rendering - ray-tracing.
courses.dp_requirements: 40% in the practical assignments for the course.
courses.assessment: Assignment (40%), Exam (60%).

courses.semester: Not specified (Z). Note: This course will not be offered every year.
courses.prerequisites: Admission into the Honours in Computer Science, or permission from the course convener. Familiarity with basic discrete mathematics is highly recommended.
courses.course_outline: Logic plays a central role in many areas of Artificial Intelligence. This course will introduce students to Propositional Logic and Description Logics: logics frequently used in the area of Knowledge Representation and Reasoning. Description Logics are frequently used to represent formal ontologies. Topics covered include: Reasoning in propositional logic with tableaux algorithms; the Description Logic ALC; Reasoning in Description Logics with tableaux algorithms.
courses.assessment: Exam: open book, 3 hours, 50%; Assignments: 50%.

courses.semester: Not specified (Z). Note: This course will not be offered every year.
courses.prerequisites: Admission into the Honours in Computer Science, or permission from the course convener.
courses.course_outline: This course will introduce students to the subarea of Artificial Intelligence known as Non-Monotonic Reasoning. Students will study the theoretical core of this type of reasoning and will be introduced to the state of the art in this area.
courses.assessment: 24-hour take-home exam, 50%; Assignments: 50%.

courses.semester: Not specified (Z). Note: This course will not be offered every year.
courses.prerequisites: Admission into the Honours in Computer Science, or permission from the course convener.
courses.course_outline: This course will introduce students to the formalism of logic programming, allowing them to model and solve problems in a fragment of first order logic. The course will touch on theoretical foundations as well as implementations and applications.
courses.assessment: Exam: 24-hour take-home exam, 50%; Assignments: 50%.

courses.semester: Not specified (Z). Note: This course will not be offered every year.
courses.prerequisites: Admission into the Honours in Computer Science, or permission from the course convener.
courses.course_outline: Evolutionary computation is a sub-field of biologically inspired artificial intelligence and general algorithmic frameworks applicable to different optimisation problems. This course examines the theory and application of several evolutionary algorithms, including: genetic algorithms, genetic programming and evolutionary strategies. Course objectives include: gaining an understanding of the algorithmic theory and implementation of various evolutionary algorithms, identifying evolutionary algorithms suitable for solving different types of problems, and how to apply such evolutionary algorithms to optimisation, machine learning, and design tasks.
courses.assessment: Exam: closed book, 2 hours, 60%; Practical assignment: 40%.

courses.semester: Not specified (Z). Note: This course will not be offered every year.
courses.prerequisites: Admission into the Honours in Computer Science, or permission from the course convener.
courses.course_outline: Artificial life is a multi-disciplinary field of study (drawing from computer science, artificial intelligence, complex systems, evolutionary biology and chemistry), wherein researchers examine living systems, their adaptive processes and evolution. This course examines the origins of ALIFE, Cybernetics and AI: simulation and synthesis of lifelike systems, self-reproducing artificial evolution systems of digital organisms, cellular automata, swarm intelligence and stigmergy, as well as nature-inspired computation such as automated product design and evolutionary art. Course objectives include gaining an understanding of the theory and application of several selected topics via the implementation and analysis of various artificial life systems.
courses.assessment: Exam: 50%; Practical Work: 50%.

courses.semester: Full Year (W)
courses.prerequisites: Computer Science Honours from UCT prior to 2018, or permission from the Head of Department in exceptional cases. In the normal case, students will be expected to register for Master's specialising in Computer Science by coursework and minor dissertation. Students will be expected to attend a research methods course in the first year.
courses.course_outline: This course consists of an investigation of an approved topic chosen for intensive study by the candidate, culminating in the submission of a dissertation. The dissertation shall demonstrate the successful completion of a programme of training in research methods, a thorough understanding of the scientific principles underlying the research and an appropriate acquaintance with the relevant literature. It must be clearly presented and conform to the standards of the department and faculty.
courses.assessment: Examination by dissertation.

courses.year_level: Postgraduate (Master's by coursework and minor dissertation)
courses.semester: Full Year (W)
courses.prerequisites: Completion of all coursework, or permission of the convener. Students must have completed the Research Methods course and at least four (out of six) elective courses before registering.
courses.course_outline: Upon successful completion of the coursework, students will be required to register for this minor dissertation component and complete a suitable research project under supervision of an appropriate computer science academic staff member. The research component will expose the student to research methodology, experimental design, data analysis techniques, and dissertation writing skills.
courses.assessment: The minor dissertation must be presented for formal examination. The coursework and minor dissertation each count 50% towards the degree; each must be passed separately for the award of the degree.

courses.year_level: Postgraduate (Master's by coursework and minor dissertation)
courses.semester: Full Year (W)
courses.prerequisites: Completion of all coursework, or permission of the convener.
courses.course_outline: Upon successful completion of the coursework component, students will be required to register for this minor dissertation component in Artificial Intelligence and complete a suitable research project on a topic within the broad area of Artificial Intelligence under supervision of an appropriate computer science academic staff member. The research component will expose the student to research methodology, experimental design, data analysis techniques, and dissertation writing skills.
courses.assessment: The minor dissertation must be presented for formal examination. The coursework and minor dissertation each count 50% towards the degree; each must be passed separately for the award of the degree.

courses.semester: Full Year (W)
courses.prerequisites: CSC5005H and CSC5006H, or (CSC5007Z/CSC5013Z, CSC5010Z, CSC5011Z, CSC5012Z, CSC5014Z/INF5007Z, CSC5015Z/INF5009Z, CSC5016Z/INF5008Z and CSC5017Z), or permission from the convener.
courses.course_outline: Upon successful completion of the coursework component, students will be required to register for this minor dissertation course and complete a one-year research project under supervision of an appropriate computer science academic staff member.
courses.assessment: The minor dissertation must be presented for formal examination. The coursework and minor dissertation each count 50% towards the degree; each must be passed separately for the award of the degree.

courses.semester: Not specified (Z)
courses.prerequisites: Acceptance into the Master's degree specialising in Data Science or Information Technology.
courses.course_outline: This course introduces students with little or no prior experience to the three cornerstone database technologies for big data: relational, NoSQL and Hadoop ecosystems. The relational part covers conceptual, logical and physical database design including ER modelling and normalisation theory, SQL coding and performance enhancement best practices. NoSQL databases for big data and semi-structured data applications are introduced across all four architectural types. The final section covers Hadoop technologies for distributed data processing such as MapReduce programming and Apache Spark. The course is presented in an online format.
courses.assessment: Final examination: 50%; Practical assignments: 50%. A sub-minimum of 40% will be required for each of the assignment and exam components of the course.

courses.semester: Not specified (Z). Note: This course will not be offered every year.
courses.prerequisites: Admission into the Master's degree specialising in Computer Science, or permission from the course convener.
courses.course_outline: Visualisation is the graphical representation of data with the goal of improving comprehension, communication, hypothesis generation and decision making. This course aims to teach the principles of effective visualisation of large, multidimensional data sets. We cover the field of visual thinking, outlining current understanding of human perception and demonstrating how we can use this knowledge to create more effective data visualisations.
courses.assessment: Students will be assessed with assignments (50%) and an exam (50%). A sub-minimum of 40% will be required for each of the assignment and exam components of the course.

courses.semester: Full Year (W)
courses.prerequisites: Successful completion of the coursework component of the Master's specialising in Data Science.
courses.course_outline: The research component of the degree is based on a 90 credit dissertation. The topic of the research will be based on an analysis of large data sets from Physics, Astronomy, Medicine, Finance or other areas of application using methodology learnt in the coursework component. Alternatively, the dissertation component may focus on methodological developments in Computer Sciences required for the analysis of large amounts of data.
courses.assessment: Examination by dissertation.

courses.semester: Not specified (Z)
courses.prerequisites: Admission into the Master's degree specialising in IT, or permission from the course convener.
courses.course_outline: A framework for describing the operation of computer networks is developed. Starting with local-area networks, packet-switched networks and the Internet, the module then moves to the uses made of these networks, concentrating on business applications. The effect on organisations of introducing such networked applications is also examined.
courses.assessment: Final examination: 50%; Practical assignments: 50%. A sub-minimum of 40% will be required for each of the assignment and exam components of the course.

courses.semester: Not specified (Z)
courses.prerequisites: Admission into the Master's degree specialising in IT, or permission from the course convener.
courses.course_outline: A basic introduction to object-oriented programming in Python. Python is used as an effective means of introducing programming concepts to those who are new to programming. Students will be taught how to create simple applications in the Python language.
courses.assessment: Final examination: 50%; Practical assignments: 50%. A sub-minimum of 40% will be required for each of the assignment and exam components of the course.

courses.semester: Not specified (Z)
courses.prerequisites: Admission into the Master's degree specialising in IT, or permission from the course convener.
courses.course_outline: Introduction to the discipline of human-computer interaction. This module covers how knowledge from fields such as psychology and graphic design can be used to increase the usability of computer software.
courses.assessment: Final examination: 50%; Practical assignments: 50%. A sub-minimum of 40% will be required for each of the assignment and exam components of the course.

courses.semester: Not specified (Z)
courses.prerequisites: Admission into the Master's degree specialising in IT, or permission from the course convener.
courses.course_outline: This module is intended to provide students with the insight and techniques required to allow them to write a successful postgraduate research project. Topics covered include: Introduction to IT Research; Ethics in Research; Conducting a Literature Review; Finding a Research Question/Goal; Project Management; Research Proposals; Experimentation; Prototypes; Case Studies; Surveys; Conducting Observations; Testing in IT Research; Modelling; Usability Analysis; Introduction to Statistics; The Writing Process; Research Presentations; and The Masters/PhD Thesis.
courses.assessment: Final examination: 50%; Practical assignments: 50%. A sub-minimum of 40% will be required for each of the assignment and exam components of the course.

courses.semester: Not specified (Z)
courses.prerequisites: Admission into the Master's degree specialising in Computer Science, or permission from the course convener.
courses.course_outline: The objective of the Research Methods course is to introduce students to a suite of research methods from the perspective of Computer Science, that will prepare them for the minor dissertation component of the degree. Course content includes: An introduction to finding and reading research papers; Literature reviews; Writing research proposals; Problem statements, research questions, and hypotheses; Types of research within Computer Science; Research Ethics within Computer Science; Scientific and technical writing; Qualitative and quantitative research methods; Research statistics; Research planning and grant writing; Academic career planning.
courses.assessment: A submitted literature review (50%) and research proposal (50%).

courses.semester: Not specified (Z). Note: This course will not be offered every year.
courses.prerequisites: Admission into the Master's degree specialising in Computer Science, or permission from the course convener. Computer Graphics at third-year level.
courses.course_outline: The objective is to master surface and volumetric modelling concepts applicable to 3D printing. Topics covered include: Geometry and Topology for Computer Graphics; 3D Printing Concepts (Printing Hardware, Overhang Support, Applications); Volumetric Concepts (Voxels, Computational Solid Geometry, Isosurface Extraction); Surface Concepts (Parametric Surfaces, Mesh Smoothing, Free-Form Deformation). From a practical perspective, students will code modelling software, then design and ultimately print a 3D model.
courses.assessment: Exam: open book, 2 hours, 40%; Practical assessments 50%; Final printed show piece, 10%.

courses.semester: Not specified (Z). Note: This course will not be offered every year.
courses.prerequisites: Admission into the Master's degree specialising in Computer Science, or permission from the course convener. A basic understanding of computer networking and software systems.
courses.course_outline: The objective is to provide an understanding of the basic components used to build Grid and Cloud computing systems, with a focus on how these can support Scientific Computing. This course gives an overview of the components that make up Grid and Cloud computing environments, including the components used to build distributed data and computing grids and the various 'as a Service' systems referred to as Cloud computing. It also looks at how these are used for large scale Scientific Computing.
courses.assessment: Final examination: 60%; Practical assignments: 40%.

courses.semester: Not specified (Z). Note: This course may not be offered every year.
courses.prerequisites: Admission into the Master's degree specialising in Computer Science or Artificial Intelligence, or permission from the course convener. A basic understanding of genetics and evolution is useful but not required.
courses.course_outline: Meta-heuristics are a sub-field of biologically inspired artificial intelligence and general algorithmic frameworks applicable to different optimisation problems. This course examines the theory and application of several meta-heuristic methods, including: iterated local search, tabu search, evolutionary algorithms, ant colony optimisation, simulated annealing, and particle swarm optimisation. Course objectives include gaining an understanding of the algorithmic theory and implementation of various meta-heuristics, identifying suitable meta-heuristics for solving different types of problems, and how to apply them to optimisation, machine learning, and design tasks.
courses.assessment: Exam: 50%; Practical assignments: 50%.

courses.semester: Not specified (Z). Note: This course will not be offered every year.
courses.prerequisites: Admission into the Master's degree specialising in Computer Science, or permission from the course convener. Basic understanding of XML data required. Some background in statistics and linear algebra will be useful.
courses.course_outline: The objective is to understand how search engines work at an algorithmic level and learn how to build and incorporate basic and specialised search engines into projects. Course content includes: Introduction to Information Retrieval; Models of Basic IR (Boolean, Vector, Probabilistic); IR evaluation and testbeds; Stemming, Stopping, Relevance Feedback; Models of Web and linked-data retrieval (Pagerank, HITS); Latent Semantic Analysis and Clustering; Multimedia IR; Cross-lingual and multilingual IR; and IR in Practice. Selected topics may include Distributed and Federated IR, Recommender Systems, NLP for IR, Sentiment Analysis, and Text Summarization.
courses.assessment: Exam (take-home): 40%; Assignments: 40%; Class participation: 20%.

courses.semester: Not specified (Z). Note: This course will not be offered every year.
courses.prerequisites: Admission into the Master's degree specialising in Computer Science or Artificial Intelligence, or permission from the course convener. A strong mathematics background.
courses.course_outline: This course provides an introduction to designing and implementing intelligent systems using selected Artificial Intelligence techniques, including machine learning and Bayesian Artificial Intelligence. Students learn these techniques from a Computer Science perspective, specifically how to design real world intelligent systems that incorporate such AI techniques.
courses.assessment: 2-hour open book exam: 50%; Practical assignments: 50%.

courses.semester: Not specified (Z). Note: This course will not be offered every year.
courses.prerequisites: Admission into the Master's degree specialising in Computer Science, or permission from the course convener.
courses.course_outline: The goal is to understand basic ideas underlying ICT4D and how they are used in practice. Course content includes: Introduction to key terminology around socio-economic development; Key concepts in ICT4D (e.g. social inclusion, after access); Case studies in specific domains including healthcare, agriculture, mobile money, education, etc.; Critical evaluation of ICT4D projects.
courses.assessment: Practical assignments: 80%; Case Study Presentation: 10%; Class Participation: 10%.

courses.semester: Not specified (Z). Note: This course will not be offered every year.
courses.prerequisites: Admission into the Master's degree specialising in Computer Science or Artificial Intelligence, or permission from the course convener. Familiarity with basic discrete mathematics is highly recommended.
courses.course_outline: This course will introduce students to logics used in the area of Knowledge Representation — a subarea of Artificial Intelligence. Topics covered include: The Description Logic ALC; Reasoning in Description Logics with Tableaux Algorithms; Reasoning in the EL family of Description Logics; and Query Answering.
courses.assessment: Exam: open book, 3 hours, 50%; Assignments: 50%.

courses.semester: Not specified (Z). Note: This course will NOT be offered in 2026.
courses.prerequisites: Admission into the Master's degree specialising in Computer Science or Artificial Intelligence, or permission from the course convener. Experience in modelling (ER, UML Class diagrams) and some familiarity with logic will be helpful.
courses.course_outline: The principal aim is to provide participants with an overview of ontology engineering — including language features, automated reasoning, and top-down and bottom-up ontology development — and a main application field being the Semantic Web. Topics covered include: Logic foundations for ontologies (Languages: Description Logics, OWL; Automated reasoning: class and instance classification, satisfiability and ontology consistency checking); Ontology development (top-down using foundational ontologies and design patterns; bottom-up exploiting legacy material such as relational databases, thesauri, text; methodologies for ontology development and maintenance).
courses.assessment: Exam (closed-book but with some material provided) - 50%; Assignments - 50%.

courses.course_title: Introduction to Image Processing and Computer Vision
courses.semester: Not specified (Z). Note: This course will not be offered every year.
courses.prerequisites: Admission into the Master's degree specialising in Computer Science or Artificial Intelligence, or permission from the course convener.
courses.course_outline: To introduce students to basic concepts in computer vision and image processing, oriented towards solving real world, practical image analysis problems. Topics include: Basic Signal processing; Image Transforms and Operations; Feature Detection; Object Descriptions; Basic Segmentation and Registration; Fundamental Segmentation techniques; Machine Learning and GAs in Computer Vision. Case studies and topical papers are discussed.
courses.assessment: Exam: Open Book, 2 hours. Class Record: Practical 60%, Review Questions 40%. Final Mark: Exam 40%, Class Record 60%.

courses.course_title: Advanced Topics in Computer Science Master's 1
courses.semester: Not specified (Z). Note: This course will not be offered every year.
courses.prerequisites: Admission into the Master's degree specialising in Computer Science or Artificial Intelligence, or permission from the course convener.
courses.course_outline: This course introduces advanced and cutting edge topics in Computer Science as they emerge with new areas of investigation or practice.
courses.assessment: Exam: 50% and Coursework: 50%.

courses.course_title: Advanced Topics in Computer Science Master's 2
courses.semester: Not specified (Z). Note: This course will not be offered every year.
courses.prerequisites: Admission into the Master's degree specialising in Computer Science or Artificial Intelligence, or permission from the course convener.
courses.course_outline: To introduce advanced and cutting edge topics in Computer Science as they emerge as new areas of investigation or practice.
courses.assessment: Exam: 50% and Coursework: 50%.

courses.semester: Not specified (Z). Note: This course will not be offered every year.
courses.prerequisites: Admission into the Master's degree specialising in Computer Science, or permission from the course convener. Working knowledge of computer networks.
courses.course_outline: The objective is to gain advanced understanding of techniques for traffic engineering and quality of service in the Internet architecture. Course content includes: New Network and Transport Protocols (IPv6, Mobile IP, IP Multicast, Multipath TCP, QUIC); Routing and Traffic Engineering; Traffic Engineering with Overlay Networking (MPLS/GMPLS, SDN, NFV); Internet Measurements (QoS and QoE, IP Traffic Monitoring and Analysis). Selected topics may include Cloud Infrastructure, Content Delivery Networks, Internet Access in the Developing World, and Online Data Protection.
courses.assessment: Assignments: 40%; Discussion sessions: 15%; Active Participation in Class: 5%; Final Exam: 40%.

courses.semester: Not specified (Z). Note: This course will not be offered every year.
courses.prerequisites: Admission into the Master's degree specialising in Computer Science, or permission from the course convener.
courses.course_outline: This course introduces basic concepts and practice around user-centred design of digital systems. Topics include the design cycle, sketching and storyboarding, task analysis, contextual inquiry, conceptual models, usability inspection, human information processing, experience design, and qualitative and quantitative study design and evaluation. The course contains additional practical work to distinguish it from the honours level module CSC4024Z.
courses.assessment: Participation: 10%; Individual Practical Assessments: 20%; Group Project Assessments: 40%; Final Exam: 30%.

courses.semester: First Semester (Z). Note: This course will not be offered every year.
courses.prerequisites: Admission into the Master's degree specialising in Artificial Intelligence, or permission from the course convener.
courses.course_outline: This course will expose students to foundational concepts and computational techniques in Machine Learning and underlying theory related to formulating and implementing machine learning algorithms to solve a wide range of problems. Students will also learn how to implement a broad range of classical to biologically inspired machine learning algorithms with real-world applications. Core topics include supervised and unsupervised learning such as: concept learning, clustering, artificial neural networks and reinforcement learning.
courses.assessment: Practical work counts 50%; examination counts 50%.

courses.semester: Not specified (Z). Note: This course will not be offered every year.
courses.prerequisites: Admission into the Master's degree specialising in Computer Science or Artificial Intelligence, or permission from the course convener.
courses.course_outline: The course introduces fundamental concepts and current approaches in Natural Language Processing. Course content includes: Text preprocessing; Naive Bayes and logistic regression for text classification; Word vectors and distributional semantics; n-gram language models; Sequence labelling with hidden Markov Models; Syntactic parsing; Recurrent neural networks for sequence processing; Encoder-decoder neural networks; Transformers neural networks and contextual embeddings. Selected NLP applications include: Sentiment analysis, Parts-of-Speech tagging, Named Entity Recognition, machine translation, information extraction and question answering.
courses.assessment: Exam (take-home): 50%; Assignments: 50%.

courses.semester: Not specified (Z). Note: This course will not be offered every year.
courses.prerequisites: Admission into the Master's degree specialising in Computer Science or Artificial Intelligence, or permission from the course convener.
courses.course_outline: Virtual Reality (VR) involves the creation of a digital replacement for the senses using devices such as head-mounted displays and haptic feedback. VR has applications in games, simulation and training. This course introduces the theoretical underpinnings and practical skills necessary for creating virtual environments. Topics covered include: Interaction in VR, navigation and locomotion in VR, simulator sickness, immersion and presence, designing VR Environments with Unity, non-visual modalities (binaural output, virtual flavour, haptics).
courses.assessment: Exam: open book, 24 hours, 50%; Assignments: 50%.

courses.semester: Not specified (Z). Note: This course will not be offered every year.
courses.prerequisites: Admission into the Master's degree specialising in Computer Science or Artificial Intelligence, or permission from the course convener.
courses.course_outline: This course examines the origins of ALIFE, Cybernetics and AI: simulation and synthesis of life-like systems, self-reproducing artificial evolution systems of digital organisms, cellular automata, swarm intelligence and stigmergy, as well as nature-inspired computation such as automated product design and evolutionary art. Course objectives include gaining an understanding of the theory and application of several selected topics via the implementation and analysis of various artificial life systems.
courses.assessment: Exam: 50%; Practical Work: 50%.

courses.semester: Not specified (Z). Note: This course will not be offered every year.
courses.prerequisites: Admission into the Master's degree specialising in Computer Science or Artificial Intelligence, or permission from the course convener.
courses.course_outline: This course will introduce students to the subarea of Artificial Intelligence known as Non-Monotonic Reasoning. In addition to a brief history of the topic, students will be given a detailed introduction to at least one of the widely used approaches. Students will learn these approaches from both a theoretical and computational perspective.
courses.assessment: 24-hour take-home exam, 50%; Assignments: 50%.

courses.semester: Not specified (Z). Note: This course will not be offered every year.
courses.prerequisites: Admission into the Master's degree specialising in Computer Science or Artificial Intelligence, or permission from the course convener.
courses.course_outline: This course will introduce students to the subarea of Artificial Intelligence known as Natural Language Generation (NLG). In addition to a brief history of the topic, students will be given a detailed introduction to three approaches for building NLG systems: symbolic, statistical, and neural techniques. Students will learn these approaches from both a theoretical and computational perspective.
courses.assessment: Exam: 24-hour take-home exam, 60%; Assignments: 40%.

courses.course_title: Masters in IT: Information Technology Minor Dissertation
courses.semester: Full Year (W)
courses.prerequisites: A non-IT honours degree, or equivalent.
courses.course_outline: The research component will expose students to critical reading and writing skills, develop analysis and research skills through research methodology and data analysis techniques. Students should be in a position to submit the final dissertation by the end of the year.
courses.dp_requirements: In order to be permitted to sit the final exam a minimum average of 40% on the coursework must be obtained.
courses.assessment: The minor dissertation must be presented for formal examination. The coursework and minor dissertation each count 50% towards the degree; each must be passed separately for the award of the degree.

courses.semester: Not specified (Z)
courses.prerequisites: A non-IT honours degree, or equivalent.
courses.course_outline: Course objectives: 1) Identify important social and human factor issues that impact professional behaviour linked to Cyber Security. 2) Research and analyse material and real-world situations that relate to social and human factor issues linked to Cyber Security. 3) Discuss and report the outcomes of investigations. 4) Provide advice and recommendations about how to tackle social and human factor issues linked to Cyber Security.
courses.dp_requirements: In order to be permitted to sit the final exam a minimum average of 40% on the coursework must be obtained.
courses.assessment: 2 Assignments (50%) and an Exam (50%).

courses.semester: Not specified (Z)
courses.prerequisites: A non-IT honours degree, or equivalent.
courses.course_outline: The course covers: Cybersecurity and impacting issues; Cyber security theories and frameworks; Real-world Cybercrime Cases; Cyber-Forensic investigations; Reporting and evidence admissibility; Strategies to address Cyber security challenges.
courses.dp_requirements: In order to be permitted to sit the final exam a minimum average of 40% on the coursework must be obtained.
courses.assessment: 2 Assignments (50%) and an Exam (50%).

courses.semester: Not specified (Z)
courses.prerequisites: A non-IT honours degree, or equivalent.
courses.course_outline: This module aims to introduce a range of techniques within both structured and object-oriented methods, in order to enable students to analyse and design well engineered software solutions. Students will be introduced to the practical use of CASE tools in modelling and documenting analysis and design specifications. Different life cycle models will also be discussed.
courses.dp_requirements: In order to be permitted to sit the final exam a minimum average of 40% on the coursework must be obtained.
courses.assessment: 2 Assignments (50%) and an Exam (50%).

courses.semester: Full Year (W)
courses.prerequisites: Not specified. Students will be expected to attend a research methods course in the first year.
courses.course_outline: The PhD is a research degree on an advanced topic under supervision. Examination is by thesis alone. A candidate shall undertake doctoral research and advanced study under the guidance of a supervisor/s appointed by Senate. The thesis must constitute a substantial contribution to knowledge in the chosen subject, must show evidence of original investigation and give a full statement of the literature on the subject. The PhD degree demands that the candidate is able to conduct independent research on their own initiative. Through the thesis the candidate must demonstrate that they are at the academic forefront in the topic selected, that the work is original and that it advances knowledge in the relevant field. Candidates are referred to the rules for this degree as set out in Book 3, General Rules and Policies.
courses.assessment: Examination by thesis alone.
